name: always_valid
reason: Would mean every endpoint is considered valid, making the check vacuous

name: never_valid
reason: Would mean no endpoint is ever valid, making the system completely unusable

name: boundary_inclusive_at_limit
code:
proof fn phi_3_boundary_inclusive_at_limit(ep: AbstractEndPoint)
    requires
        ep.id.len() == 0x100000,
    ensures
        ep.valid_physical_address(),
{
}
reason: Off-by-one error — an id of exactly 0x100000 bytes should be rejected by the strict < bound

name: empty_id_invalid
code:
proof fn phi_4_empty_id_invalid(ep: AbstractEndPoint)
    requires
        ep.id.len() == 0,
    ensures
        !ep.valid_physical_address(),
{
}
reason: Would mean a zero-length endpoint id is not a valid address, contradicting 0 < 0x100000

name: valid_implies_very_short
code:
proof fn phi_5_valid_implies_very_short(ep: AbstractEndPoint)
    requires
        ep.valid_physical_address(),
    ensures
        ep.id.len() < 256,
{
}
reason: Would mean the spec is far more restrictive than intended, capping ids at 256 bytes instead of ~1MB
